strengths: Novel idea: applies truth maintenance systems to agent memory for temporal consistency.
Clear problem framing: identifies truth maintenance as a key gap in long-horizon agent memory.
Proposes a structured claim schema with validity intervals and explicit contradiction/supersede edges.
Reports strong gains on specific slices (abstention, single-session-user) with strict dominance claims.
Includes an ablation study to isolate the contribution of the LLM linker.
weaknesses: Overall accuracy is lower than the strong baseline (61.3% vs 68.0%), raising questions about general utility.
Experimental results are incomplete: key numbers (e.g., temporal-reasoning, multi-session) are missing from the main table.
Method details are vague: no specifics on how the LLM linker is implemented or how the 'maximum consistent subgraph' algorithm is justified.
Evaluation scale is limited: only a 150-question slice of LongMemEval-S is used, not the full benchmark.
Claims of 'strict dominance' are based on very small sample sizes (n=9, n=21), limiting statistical confidence.
Lack of comparison to recent state-of-the-art memory systems beyond a reimplemented A-Mem.
required_revisions: Provide the complete results table (Table 1) with all six LongMemEval-S question types and the overall accuracy for all baselines.
Clarify the implementation of the LLM linker: prompt details, model used, and how confidence thresholds are set.
Justify the greedy 'maximum consistent subgraph' algorithm with theoretical or empirical evidence for near-optimality.
Report standard deviations or confidence intervals for accuracy scores across the three random seeds.
Include a comparison to a more diverse set of recent memory baselines (e.g., MemGPT, MemoryBank).
Explain the drop in performance on non-target slices (e.g., assistant-authored, multi-session) and discuss trade-offs.
Provide the exact prompts used for the writer, linker, and parser in the main text or a clearly referenced appendix.

strengths: Novel idea: applies truth maintenance to agent memory via claim-level graph
Clear problem framing: focuses on truthfulness and abstention in long-horizon memory
Strong ablation: isolates causal role of LLM linker via -contradict ablation
Efficiency claim: reports lower token cost vs. baseline A-Mem
weaknesses: Overall accuracy lower than baseline (61.3% vs 68.0%)
Small sample sizes (n=9 for abstention, n=21 for SSU) limit statistical confidence
Missing standard error bars or significance tests for most results
No comparison to recent state-of-the-art memory systems beyond A-Mem
Failure analysis limited; no breakdown of error types on underperforming slices
required_revisions: Report standard deviations or confidence intervals for all accuracy numbers
Conduct proper statistical significance tests (e.g., paired t-test, bootstrap) for all comparisons
Clarify how the 150-question stratified slice was selected from LongMemEval-S
Justify why overall accuracy is lower despite gains on specific slices
Include a baseline that uses raw turns + claim schema but no linker (to separate schema vs. linker effects)
Provide full results table with all six LongMemEval-S question types, not just selected slices
Clarify token counting methodology: are tokens per question averaged over ingestion + query?
Specify the exact version of DeepSeek-V3.2 used and any prompting details not in appendix

strengths: Clear problem framing: truth maintenance for long-horizon agents
Novel integration of truth maintenance systems (TMS) with LLM-based memory
Strong empirical results on abstention and single-session-user slices
Efficiency claims: lower token cost than A-Mem baseline
Well-defined claim schema and edge types (support, contradict, supersede)
weaknesses: Overall accuracy (61.3%) is lower than baseline (68.0%)
Performance on key target slice (temporal reasoning) only matches baseline
No latency breakdown (ingestion vs. query) for system assessment
Scalability of O(k^2) maximum-consistent subgraph algorithm not analyzed
Ablation shows linker is critical, but schema alone underperforms
required_revisions: Clarify how 'strictly dominates' is defined given lower overall accuracy
Provide latency breakdown (write-time vs. read-time) for practical assessment
Justify hyperparameter choices (similarity gate, bypass threshold) or show sensitivity
Include placeholder numbers in tables (e.g., Table 1 F1, Tokens/q) with real values
Discuss failure modes: why do assistant/multi-session slices regress?
